title: Mitigating Privacy Risk of Adversarial Examples with Counterfactual Explanations

reviews.summary: This paper proposes to generate adversarial examples via counterfactual explanations in an autoencoder latent space, guided by class prototypes and a sparsity objective, with the goal of mitigating privacy risks (membership inference attacks) while maintaining model robustness and accuracy. The authors decouple adversarial example generation from training, argue that counterfactuals produce in-distribution, semantic perturbations, and report near-chance MIA accuracy on MNIST. They further explore the trade-off among accuracy, robustness, and privacy by varying the proportion of adversarial examples used during training.
strengths: Using prototype-guided counterfactual explanations in an autoencoder latent space to craft adversarial examples is an interesting angle that tries to align perturbations with data manifold structure.
The conceptual analysis connecting counterfactual explanations and adversarial examples is timely and may inspire cross-fertilization between explainability and adversarial robustness.
The paper evaluates membership inference attack (MIA) success rates for different adversarial-example generation methods and reports chance-level MIA on MNIST for the proposed approach.
Attempts are made to control for some confounders (e.g., decoupling generation from training across all compared methods).
The high-level motivation—reducing the generalization gap and aligning adversarial examples with the data distribution to lower privacy leakage—is clear and well-motivated.
The qualitative t-SNE analyses offer an intuitive perspective on distributional differences among datasets.

Addressing the tension between robustness and privacy is an important and underexplored topic, and the idea of counterfactual-based in-distribution adversarial training could be impactful if substantiated with stronger evidence.
weaknesses: Key parts of the method are under-specified. Equations (1)–(3) do not define the optimization variables clearly, the role of the nearest neighbor and prototype target are inconsistent across sections, and the loss terms L_pred, L_AE, L_sparse, L_proto are not precisely defined in the main text.

The claim that counterfactual-based adversarial examples are “imperceptible” is not supported by formal constraints (e.g., Lp norm budgets) or perceptual metrics; MNIST perturbation norms/budgets are not reported.

Decoupling adversarial example generation from training may significantly weaken robustness relative to standard adversarial training (e.g., Madry-style inner maximization), but the paper does not assess actual adversarial robustness of the trained models.
Experiments are limited to MNIST with one small CNN; no evaluation on more challenging datasets (e.g., CIFAR-10/100, SVHN) or architectures.

No robust accuracy is reported under strong attacks (e.g., multi-step PGD, AutoAttack) at standard threat models; “robustness” is effectively proxied by the fraction of adversarial examples used in training, which is insufficient.

The MIA evaluation lacks critical details (attack variant, features used, shadow-model setup, calibration) and appears to test only black-box confidence-based attacks; stronger/broader MIAs (e.g., loss-trajectory, label-only, white-box) are not considered.
The training set sizes differ across methods (e.g., AdvGAN produces far fewer examples), which can confound both utility and privacy results; controls for sample-size effects are insufficient.
Notation and descriptions are often ambiguous (e.g., what exactly is minimized in Eq. (2)? how are the prototypes computed? which class is targeted during cf(x_nb)?).

Some claims contradict common practice (e.g., visible perturbations on MNIST are typically due to chosen epsilon; the definition of “imperceptible” is not universal), and figures/tables have formatting artifacts that hinder interpretation.

The paper does not engage with a sizable body of work on in-distribution/latent-space or semantic adversarial examples, adversarial perturbations of deep representations, and prototype-guided or manifold-based adversarial training.
No comparisons to strong robust training baselines (e.g., TRADES) or modern evaluation protocols (e.g., AutoAttack) are provided.
reviews.questions:
1. Please provide a precise algorithm/pseudocode for generating x_adv from x, including how x_nb is selected, what class is targeted in cf(x_nb), and the exact definitions and weights of L_pred, L_AE, L_sparse, and L_proto.
2. What is the exact MIA setup? Which attack features were used (probabilities, logits, losses, labels), was any calibration employed, and were shadow models trained? Is the MIA predicting membership in the adversarial training set or in the original clean set?
3. What are the threat-model parameters for robustness (norm, epsilon, steps)? Please report adversarial test accuracy under strong attacks (multi-step PGD and AutoAttack) at standard MNIST budgets, and ensure transfer/gradient-free attacks are included to rule out obfuscated gradients.
4. How are class prototypes computed in practice (in input space vs. latent space)? Are they fixed after autoencoder training or updated during CF optimization? Please include ablations showing the effect of removing each loss term on MIA and accuracy.
5. Why does AdvGAN yield far fewer adversarial examples? How do you control for the impact of differing dataset sizes on both utility and privacy? Can you standardize the number of training examples across methods and rerun the comparisons?
6. The claim that typical MNIST perturbations from PGD/AdvGAN are “inconsistent with the definition of adversarial examples” seems to conflate epsilon choices with imperceptibility. What epsilon values and norms were used for baselines, and how do your perturbation norms compare?
7. Have you evaluated on more challenging datasets (e.g., CIFAR-10) and larger architectures? If not, can you discuss expected scalability and provide preliminary results?
reviews.assessment: The paper tackles an important and timely problem—reconciling adversarial robustness and privacy—via an intriguing idea: using prototype-guided counterfactual explanations in a latent space to craft in-distribution adversarial examples that reduce membership inference risk. However, in its current form, the work falls short of ICLR standards. The method is under-specified, several claims (imperceptibility, “first use” of counterfactuals for adversarial generation, ideal balance at 95% adversarial training data) are not convincingly substantiated, and the experimental evaluation is limited (single dataset, no robust accuracy under strong attacks, incomplete privacy assessment, confounded comparisons due to different training set sizes). The t-SNE visualizations and near-chance MIA accuracy on MNIST are encouraging but insufficient. With clearer methodological detail, standardized and stronger baselines, comprehensive robustness and privacy evaluations, and experiments beyond MNIST, this line of work could become impactful. As it stands, I recommend rejection, with the hope that a revised version can address these substantial concerns.
